coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Co(dmgH)2PyCl synergistically promote photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over hexagonal ZnIn2S4
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Yao
Gao, Yanhong
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites (CQDs stands for carbon quantum dot) prepared by a microwave method in the presence of performed CQDs were impregnated with Co(dmgH)2PyCl (dmgH = dimethylglyoxime, Py = pyridine) to form ternary Co(dmgH)2PyCl/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites (Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4). The photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution in the presence of TEOA as a sacrificial agent over the as-obtained Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 under visible light was investigated. It was found that ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 show significantly superior photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution than both Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and CQDs/ZnIn2S4. An optimum activity was realized over Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 with the loading amount of 5.0 wt% CQDs and 1.0 wt% Co(dmgH)2PyCl, in which 703.9 μmol of hydrogen was evolved in 8 h. The superior photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution over ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 can be ascribed to the synergistic effect played by CQDs and Co(dmgH)2PyCl, in which CQDs acts as a mediator to promote the transfer of the photogenerated electrons to Co(dmgH)2PyCl, the hydrogen evolution cocatalyst. This work provides some guidance for the design of noble metal free photocatalysts for hydrogen generation and highlights the potential of using CQDs to promote the charge transfer in semiconductor-based photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Synergistic effect
CQDs
Hydrogenevolution
Visible light
ZnIn2S4

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Award Program for Minjiang Scholar Professorship

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by NSFC ( U1705251 ) and 973 Programs ( 2014CB239303 ). Z. Li thanks the Award Program for Minjiang Scholar Professorship for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) pure ZnIn2S4; (b) CQDs/ZnIn2S4; (c) Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 and (d) JCPDS file of ZnIn2S4.
XPS spectra of ZnIn2S4, 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 in (a) Zn 2p region; (b) In 3d region; (c) S 2p region;(d) C 1 s region; XPS spectra of 1.0 wt%-Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 in (e) Co 2p region and (f) N 1 s region.
Images of CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite (a) SEM; (b-c) TEM and (d) HRTEM.
UV–Vis DRS spectra of (a) ZnIn2S4, (b) CQDs/ZnIn2S4 composite and (c) Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 composite (inset: UV–Vis DRS spectra of CQDs).

(a) Time-dependent photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over (1) ZnIn2S4, (2) 1.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 and (3) CQDs; (b) Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution after 8 h irradiation over (1) pure ZnIn2S4; ZnIn2S4 with different amounts of CQDs (2) 1.0 wt%; (3) 3.0 wt%; (4) 5.0 wt%; (5) 7.0 wt%; (6) 10.0 wt% (reaction conditions: catalyst, 0.05 g; 100 mL aqueous solutions containing 0.1 M TEOA).

(a) Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution after 8 h irradiation over Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 composite with different amounts of Co(dmgH)2PyCl; (b) Time-dependent hydrogen evolution over (1) pure ZnIn2S4, (2) CQDs/ZnIn2S4, (3) Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and (4) 1.0 wt%-Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4.

(a) Photocurrent responses of ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4. nanocomposite, with an ac bias of 0 V vs Ag/AgCl under simulate light illumination, Na2SO4 aqueous solution (0.1 M) was used as the electrolyte; (b) Nyquist plots of experimental impedance data for ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite in the frequency range of 0.1–1.0 × 105 Hz using an ac bias of 0 V vs Ag/AgCl without visible light irradiations in an aqueous solution containing K3[Fe(CN)6] (1 mmol/L), K4[Fe(CN)6] (1 mmol/L) and KCl (0.1 mol/L).
Cycling of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over 1.0 wt%-Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4.
Proposed mechanism for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Co(dmgH)2PyCl synergistically promote photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over hexagonal ZnIn2S4

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Photocatalysis, State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites (CQDs stands for carbon quantum dot) prepared by a microwave method in the presence of performed CQDs were impregnated with Co(dmgH)2PyCl (dmgH = dimethylglyoxime, Py = pyridine) to form ternary Co(dmgH)2PyCl/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites (Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4). The photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution in the presence of TEOA as a sacrificial agent over the as-obtained Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 under visible light was investigated. It was found that ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 show significantly superior photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution than both Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and CQDs/ZnIn2S4. An optimum activity was realized over Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 with the loading amount of 5.0 wt% CQDs and 1.0 wt% Co(dmgH)2PyCl, in which 703.9 μmol of hydrogen was evolved in 8 h. The superior photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution over ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 can be ascribed to the synergistic effect played by CQDs and Co(dmgH)2PyCl, in which CQDs acts as a mediator to promote the transfer of the photogenerated electrons to Co(dmgH)2PyCl, the hydrogen evolution cocatalyst. This work provides some guidance for the design of noble metal free photocatalysts for hydrogen generation and highlights the potential of using CQDs to promote the charge transfer in semiconductor-based photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Since hydrogen is an ideal clean fuel and an alternative to traditional fossil fuels, efficient conversion and storage of solar energy in the form of hydrogen by photocatalytic water splitting has been considered to be one of the most promising strategies to solve the worldwide energy crisis and environmental pollution [1–2]. Ever since the first demonstration that a photoelectrochemical cell using Pt–TiO2 electrodes was active for hydrogen evolution [3], great efforts have been devoted to developing highly efficient photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution [4–10]. So far, a variety of inorganic semiconductors including metal oxides [5,11–14], sulfides [4,15–18] and oxynitrides [6,19–20] as well as metal-free organic semiconductors [8,21–23] have been developed for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Among the already reported photocatalysts, ZnIn2S4, a ternary chalcogenide with a bandgap (ca. 2.4 eV) well responding to visible light [24], has been extensively studied in photocatalysis, especially in hydrogen evolution [25–26] and organic pollutant degradation [27–28]. However, ZnIn2S4 alone shows inferior performance for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution due to the short lifetime of the photo-induced carriers [29–30]. Since cocatalysts can decrease the recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carrier and lower the overpotential for hydrogen evolution by providing redox reaction sites for hydrogen evolution [2,5,31–34], different cocatalysts including noble metal nanoparticles like Pt [35], layered transition metal chalcogenides like NiS [36] and MoS2 [37–38] as well as cobaloximes complexes [39] have been deposited on ZnIn2S4 to improve its photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution.

As a noble metal free cobaloximes complex, Co(dmgH)2PyCl has long been used as an electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution. Since the reduction potentials of CoIII → CoII (−0.35 V vs. NHE) and CoII → CoI (−0.78 V vs. NHE) in CoIII(dmgH)2PyCl are less negative than the conduction band (CB) of ZnIn2S4 (ca. −1.1 V vs. NHE), Co(dmgH)2PyCl has also been used as a cocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4 [39–40]. Our previous study revealed that Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 showed even superior performance for hydrogen evolution than Pt/ZnIn2S4 [28]. However, the existence of multiple equilibriums in the two-electron transfer pathway, ie, reduction of CoIII to form CoII followed by a secondary electron-transfer to reduce CoII to form CoI, the electron transfer from the CB of ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl to form the catalytic active CoI for hydrogen evolution is sluggishly as evidenced from the presence of an induction period for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over irradiated ZnIn2S4 [41].

CQDs, monodisperse graphite particles with diameter of less than 10 nm, exhibit unique properties in light-harvesting, photo-induced electron transfer, electron reservoir and tunable photoluminescence (PL), which results in its applications in a variety of fields [42–43]. Taking advantage of its superior property for promoting charge transfer, its applications on photocatalysis has attracted recent interest [44–51]. CQDs have been incorporated with semiconductors to facilitate the separation and transportation of the photo-generated carriers over semiconductors [52–57]. It is therefore anticipated that CQDs can serve as a bridge to accelerate the electrons transfer between Co(dmgH)2PyCl and ZnIn2S4. Herein, we reported the preparation of ternary Co(dmgH)2PyCl/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4) nanocomposites, which show superior photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution under visible light in the presence of TEOA as a sacrificial agent, ascribable to the synergistic effect played by CQDs and Co(dmgH)2PyCl.

All the reagents are analytical grade and used without further purifications. Co(dmgH)2PyCl was prepared from CoCl2·6H2O, dimethylglyoxime and pyridine following a previously reported procedure [58]. CoCl2·6H2O (0.500 g, 2.15 mmol) was dissolved in ethanol (200 mL) heated in 70 °C and dimethylglyoxime (0.551 mg, 4.7 mmol) was added. Then, pyridine (0.344 g, 4.3 mmol) was added drop-wisely to the above solution with O2 continuously blew to the solution. After that, the suspension was cooled to r.t and the product was obtained by filtering and washed with deionized water, ethanol and diethyl ether, and dried at 60 °C under vacuum.

CQDs were synthesized by thermolysis of citric acid following a previous reported procedure [46]. 5.00 g of citric acid was heated under air at 180 °C for 2 h. Upon cooling, the resultant sample was dissolved in water and aqueous NaOH solution was added to neutralize the solution to pH 7. The final obtained CQDs was volumed to 500 mL and its concentration was determined to be 0.01 g/mL.

CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites with different amount of CQDs (mass ratio varied from 0.01 to 0.1) were prepared from ZnCl2, InCl3·4H2O and thioacetamide (TAA) in the presence of preformed CQDs via microwave-assisted heating. Typically, ZnCl2 (0.136 g, 1 mmol), InCl3·4H2O (0.586 g, 2 mmol) and TAA (0.300 g, 4 mmol) were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Then a certain amount of CQDs solution was added to the above solution. The resultant solution was sealed in a Teflon-lined double-walled digestion vessel. After treating the solution at a controllable temperature of 120 °C for 60 min using a microwave system (Ethos A, Milestone, Italy), the vessel was then cooled to room temperature. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol and dried at 60 °C. CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites with different amount of CQDs are denoted as x wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (x is the mass ratio of CQDs: ZnIn2S4, x = 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07 and 0.1).

To prepare ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites, 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite was chosen. A certain amount of Co(dmgH)2PyCl was dissolved in a minimum amount of methanol. Then 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (100 mg) were added to the above solution and the resultant suspension was incubated at room temperature for 12 h. The resultant products were dried at 60 °C under vacuum to obtain the final Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites. The Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites with different amount of Co(dmgH)2PyCl were denoted as x wt%-Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (mass ratio of Co(dmgH)2PyCl: 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4, x = 0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.07 and 0.1). For comparison, 1.0 wt%-Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 was also prepared following the previous reported method by impregnating Co(dmgH)2PyCl on ZnIn2S4 [39].

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα irradiations. The IR analyses were carried out on a Nicolet 670 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–visible diffraction spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the powders were obtained for the dry pressed disk samples using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Cary 500 Scan Spectrophotometers, Varian). BaSO4 was used as a reflectance standard. The diffuse reflectance spectra have been converted into its equivalent absorption spectra using the Kubelka-Munk function, F(R) = K/S = (1 − R)2/2R, where K is the absorption coefficient, R is the diffused reflectance, and S is the scattering coefficient. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system (PHI, USA) with a monochromatic Al Kα source and a charge neutralizer. All of the binding energies were referred to the C 1 s peak at 284.6 eV of the surface adventitious carbon. The SEM image was obtained on a field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-6700F). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained in a JEOL model JEM 2010 EX instrument at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The powder particles were supported on a carbon film coated on a 3 mm diameter fine-mesh copper grid. The sample suspension in ethanol was sonicated and a drop was dripped on the support film. N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were carried out on an ASAP2020M apparatus (Micro-meritics Instrument Corp., USA). The samples were degassed in vacuum at 100 °C for 10 h and then measured at 77 K. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses were measured on an electrochemical analyzer (Zahner, Germany) in a standard three-electrode system using the as-obtained sample as the working electrode (with an active area of ∼0.25 cm2), a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as a reference electrode. Impedance data were fitted with Z SimpWin software (Princeton Applied Research). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were carried out on a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4500). The PL emission spectra of all samples were obtained by excited at 225 nm.

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution experiments were carried out in a closed gas circulation and evacuation system fitted with a top Pyrex window. 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of aqueous solution containing 0.1 M TEOA as a sacrificial agent. The reaction system was irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter to provide the visible light irradiations. The temperature of the reaction system was maintained at room temperature by a flow of cooling water during the photocatalytic reaction. The amount of hydrogen evolved was determined with an on-line gas chromatography equipped with a TCD detector.

CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites with different amounts of CQDs were prepared via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method from ZnCl2, InCl3 and TAA in the presence of preformed CQDs with the dimension of 2.1 nm (Supporting Fig. S1). The XRD patterns of all the as-obtained products show 2 θ peak at values of 21.5°, 27.6°, 30.4°, 39.8°, 47.2°, 52.4° and 55.6°, in agreement with the (0 0 6), (1 0 2), (1 0 4), (1 0 8), (1 1 0), (1 1 6) and (0 2 2) crystallographic planes of hexagonal ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS-03-065-2023) (Fig. 1 ). Although no diffraction peaks corresponding to CQDs is observed, the FT-IR spectra of the products shows strong peaks at 1563 cm−1 and 1397 cm−1 corresponding to the -COO- stretching vibrations, as well as a strong peak at about 3500 cm−1 assignable to the -OH stretching vibrations of CQDs, indicating the presence of the CQDs in the nanocomposites (Supporting Fig. S2). The absence of the diffraction peak corresponding to graphitic structure of CQDs (29.9°) may be due to its possible overlap with the diffraction peak corresponding to (1 0 2) crystallographic plane of ZnIn2S4 (27.6°) as well as its high dispersion on ZnIn2S4. The presence of CQDs in the nanocomposite was also confirmed by the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 a–d). The XPS spectrum of 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite in the C 1 s region shows three peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.4 eV, ascribable to the sp2 hybridized carbon (CC), epoxy/hydroxyl carbon (CO) and carboxy carbon (OCO), respectively. In addition to peaks in C 1 s region, peaks assigned to Zn2+ 2p (1023.7 eV and 1046.8 eV), In3+ 3d (445.4 eV and 462.6 eV) and S2− 2p (161.6 eV) are also observed. However, as compared with pure ZnIn2S4, all the above peaks shift towards higher binding energy, suggesting the existence of interaction between CQDs and ZnIn2S4 in the nanocomposites. The SEM and TEM image of 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite clearly shows flower-like microspheres consist of large number of interwoven ultrathin nanosheets (Fig. 3 a and b). The morphology of CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite does not change obviously after the introduction of CQDs. An enlarged TEM image of the part illustrated by red dotted rectangle in Fig. 3b shows that the microspheres are consisted of nanosheets (Fig. 3c). In addition to clear lattice fringe spacing of 0.33 nm assigned to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of hexagonal ZnIn2S4, lattice fringe of 0.23 nm attributed to the graphitic structure of CQDs is also observed (Fig. 3d). All the characterizations indicate the successful incorporation of CQDs in the nanocomposite and there exists intimate contact between hexagonal ZnIn2S4 and CQDs. To fabricate Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl was loaded on the as-obtained CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites via an impregnation method. Although the existence of Co(dmgH)2PyCl cannot be evidenced from the FT-IR spectrum due to the overlap of the peak of CQDs (1563 cm−1) and that of Co(dmgH)2PyCl (1561 cm−1) (Supporting Fig. S2), its presence in the nanocomposite was confirmed from both the XPS and the EDX results. In addition to showing peaks at 781.7 eV and 796.9 eV, corresponding to Co 2p in Co3+ (Fig. 2e), the XPS in N 1 s region shows peaks at 399.9 eV, confirming the existence of N element (Fig. 2f). In addition, the EDX of 1.0 wt%-Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 shows the presence of elements of Co and N in the nanocomposite (Supporting Fig. S3).
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.08.006.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CQDs/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites were displayed in Fig. 4 . Although the incorporation of CQDs onto ZnIn2S4 does not obviously change the band gap of ZnIn2S4, the UV–vis DRS of CQDs/ZnIn2S4 shows a slightly enhanced absorption beyond 500 nm, attributable to the absorption of CQDs. The comparison of the UV–vis DRS of Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 with that of CQDs/ZnIn2S4 clearly indicates that the absorption in the region between 500 nm and 750 nm is due to the interaction between Co(dmgH)2PyCl and CQDs/ZnIn2S4, which was also observed in the UV–vis spectra of Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 reported previously [39].

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution experiments were first carried out over the as-prepared CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites in the presence of TEOA as sacrificial agent under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5 a(1), pure ZnIn2S4 showed a low activity, with the amount of hydrogen evolved determined to be 32.4 µmol after 8 h irradiation. Although CQDs itself is not active for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (Fig. 5a(3)), an obvious improvement of the photocatalytic activity was observed when ZnIn2S4 was incorporated with only 1.0 wt% CQDs. 79.0 µmol of hydrogen was evolved over 1.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite (Fig. 5a(2)), about 2.3 times of that over pure ZnIn2S4 under otherwise similar condition, indicating that CQDs can promote the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4. The amount of hydrogen evolved increased with the irradiation time, indicating that CQDs/ZnIn2S4 was stable during the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

The amount of CQDs in the nanocomposite also influenced its photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution (Fig. 5b). The amount of hydrogen evolution first increases with the amount of CQDs in the nanocomposite, with an optimum hydrogen evolution of 163.0 μmol in 8 h irradiation observed over 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite, 5.0 times as that over bare ZnIn2S4. A further increase in the amount of CQDs results in a decrease of the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate, which is likely due to the shading effect of CQDs. The loading of too much CQDs on the surface of ZnIn2S4 will hamper the absorption of ZnIn2S4 toward visible light. Such a phenomenon has been well observed in photocatalysis [59–60]. All these results indicate that CQDs can act as a cocatalyst to promote the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4 and an appropriate loading amount is crucial. The use of CQDs as a cocatalyst to promote photocatalytic hydrogen evolution has already been reported previously over other semiconductors [61–62]. It is proposed that when irradiated, the photogenerated electrons can transfer from ZnIn2S4 to CQDs, leading to improved electron-hole separation and as result, the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4 can be enhanced.

However, the promoting effect induced by CQDs is not as significant as compared with other co-catalysts like MoS2 [63], NiS [59] and Co(dmgH)2PyCl [39]. As a good noble metal free electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution, Co(dmgH)2PyCl has been used as an efficient cocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over a variety of semiconductor-based photocatalysts [64–65]. Our previous study revealed that Co(dmgH)2PyCl is also an active co-catalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4 since the reduction potentials of CoIII → CoII (−0.35 V vs. NHE) and CoII → CoI (−0.78 V vs. NHE) in CoIII(dmgH)2PyCl are less negative than the conduction band (CB) of ZnIn2S4 (ca. −1.1 V vs. NHE) [39–40]. However, reduction of CoIII to form CoI, the catalytic active site, is a two-electron transfer process which requires that the electron transfer from the CB of ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl should be fast enough [40–41]. Otherwise an induction period should exist in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution as evidenced in our previously reported Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 systems [39]. Considering the excellent electron-transfer properties of CQDs, it is therefore anticipated that CQDs can act as a bridge to facilitate the charge transfer from irradiated ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl. To realize this, Co(dmgH)2PyCl and CQDs should be co-deposited on ZnIn2S4 so that they can play synergistically to promote the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4.

Therefore, ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites were prepared using the preformed CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite and Co(dmgH)2PyCl via an impregnation method. Since 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite shows the best performance among all the investigated CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites, it was chosen to be impregnated with different amount of Co(dmgH)2PyCl to fabricate ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites with fixed CQD amount, but different amount of Co(dmgH)2PyCl. The photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution over these ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites was investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 6 a. The introduction of only 0.5 wt% Co(dmgH)2PyCl into CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite significantly improves the photocatalytic performance. The amount of the hydrogen evolved reaches 542.8 μmol in 8 h, almost 3.0 times as that over 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4. This is not surprising since previous studies has already revealed that Co(dmgH)2PyCl is a good cocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4 [39]. Similar to that reported previously on Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4, the amount of Co(dmgH)2PyCl also influences the photocatalytic activity over Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites and an optimum performance for hydrogen evolution was observed over Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite with 1.0 wt% Co(dmgH)2PyCl, with 703.9 μmol of hydrogen evolved in 8 h irradiation (Fig. 6b(4)). This equals to about 21.0 times as that over bare ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 6b(1), 32.4 μmol), and about 4.3 times as that over 5.0 wt%-CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 6b(2), 163.0 μmol) and 1.9 times as that over 1.0 wt%-Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 6b(3), 361.5 μmol).

Since the surface area and pore structure of a semiconductor can also influence its photocatalytic activity, N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 were also measured. The BET specific surface area of ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 were determined to be 29.13 cm3·g−1, 18.92 cm3·g−1,15.36 cm3·g−1 and 13.33 cm3·g−1, respectively (Supporting Fig. S4). The comparable specific surface area observed over these four photocatalysts suggests that their different photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution should be ascribed to other factors instead of the specific surface area.

Since the surface area and pore structure of a semiconductor can also influence its photocatalytic activity, N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 were also measured. The BET specific surface area of ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 were determined to be 29.13 cm3·g−1, 18.92 cm3·g−1,15.36 cm3·g−1 and 13.33 cm3·g−1, respectively (Supporting Fig. S4). The comparable specific surface area observed over these four photocatalysts suggests that their different photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution should be ascribed to other factors instead of the specific surface area.

Transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were carried out over ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4. In consistent with the order of the photocatalytic activity, the photocurrent increases in a order of ZnIn2S4 < CQDs/ZnIn2S4 < Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 < Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 7 a). In addition, the EIS of ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 shows the smallest semicircle among the four samples investigated, suggesting its superior electronic conductivity (Fig. 7b). Since photoluminescence (PL) spectra can be used to investigate the recombination rate of the photo-generated charger carriers, the PL spectra of pure ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 have also been investigated. The intensity of the PL peak decreases in the order of ZnIn2S4 > CQDs/ZnIn2S4 > Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 > Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4, indicating the decrease of the recombination of the charge carriers in these nanocomposites (Supporting Fig. S5). Among the four samples investigated, ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite shows the lowest PL intensity, in agreement with its highest photocatalytic activity. This suggests that the introduction of CQDs into the nanocomposites can suppress the recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers in ZnIn2S4 via promoting the charge transfer from photo-excited ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl.

Transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were carried out over ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4. In consistent with the order of the photocatalytic activity, the photocurrent increases in a order of ZnIn2S4 < CQDs/ZnIn2S4 < Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 < Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 7 a). In addition, the EIS of ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 shows the smallest semicircle among the four samples investigated, suggesting its superior electronic conductivity (Fig. 7b). Since photoluminescence (PL) spectra can be used to investigate the recombination rate of the photo-generated charger carriers, the PL spectra of pure ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 have also been investigated. The intensity of the PL peak decreases in the order of ZnIn2S4 > CQDs/ZnIn2S4 > Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 > Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4, indicating the decrease of the recombination of the charge carriers in these nanocomposites (Supporting Fig. S5). Among the four samples investigated, ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite shows the lowest PL intensity, in agreement with its highest photocatalytic activity. This suggests that the introduction of CQDs into the nanocomposites can suppress the recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers in ZnIn2S4 via promoting the charge transfer from photo-excited ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl.

Therefore, the different photocatalytic performance observed over ZnIn2S4, CQDs/ZnIn2S4, Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 can be explained as following. Bare ZnIn2S4 shows poor photocatalytic performance for hydrogen production due to the rapid recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers. Co(dmgH)2PyCl, a good electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution, can act as a cocatalyst to promote hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4. However, an induction period observed for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 implies the sluggish electron transfer from photo-excited ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl to form the catalytic active CoI for hydrogen evolution. Although CQDs can also act as a cocatalyst for hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4, it’s promoting effect on the hydrogen evolution is not as good as that of Co(dmgH)2PyCl. However, due to its good electronic conductivity, the introduction of CQDs can promote the electron transfer from photoexcited ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl to form the catalytic active CoI for hydrogen evolution. By working synergistically, the resultant ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite show much better performance for hydrogen evolution than both Co(dmgH)2PyCl/ZnIn2S4 and CQDs/ZnIn2S4.

The stability of 1.0 wt%-Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite was studied and the result was showed in Fig. 8 . No obvious loss of the activity was observed over 1.0 wt%-Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 after 4 cycles over a total reaction time of 24 h, suggesting that Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 was stable during the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

In summary, ternary Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites were successfully synthesized and their photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution under visible light was investigated. An optimum performance was achieved over Co/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 containing 1.0 wt% of Co(dmgH)2PyCl and 5.0 wt% of CQDs, with 703.9 μmol of hydrogen evolved in 8 h irradiation. The introduction of CQDs promotes the transfer of the photogenerated electron from ZnIn2S4 to Co(dmgH)2PyCl, the hydrogen evolution cocatalyst. CQDs and Co(dmgH)2PyCl work synergistically to promote the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over ZnIn2S4. This study highlights the potential of using CQDs to promote the photogenerated charge transfer in semiconductor-based photocatalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSFC (U1705251) and 973 Programs (2014CB239303). Z. Li thanks the Award Program for Minjiang Scholar Professorship for financial support.
